Toy Story é muito mais do que um marco técnico da animação, é uma história surpreendentemente madura sobre identidade e insegurança. Woody começa o filme como um personagem difícil de gostar. Ele é hipócrita, egoísta e constantemente hostil com Buzz por medo de perder seu espaço. Mas é justamente essa falha que torna seu arco interessante: ele precisa confrontar a própria insegurança antes de aprender o que significa amizade de verdade. Buzz, por outro lado, também é insuportável no início. Sua arrogância vem da convicção absoluta de que é um verdadeiro Space Ranger. Quando ele descobre que é apenas um brinquedo, o filme atinge seu ponto mais forte. A cena em que ele tenta voar e falha é devastadora, funciona claramente como uma metáfora para perda de identidade e até depressão. É o momento em que a fantasia colide com a realidade. O filme equilibra humor e profundidade emocional sem exageros, e mesmo sendo voltado ao público infantil, trata temas adultos com inteligência.

An Animation in a New Style Changed the World. That is what I said: it was the first CGI movie & it was acclaimed. Remember, this was made by Pixar: the grand one of CGI. I found a story sad when the first one gets left behind when another comes. But this time, it is not sad because it is funny, like Woody tries to overthrow Buzz Lightyear. This also got us to learn something: PLAY NICE. Woody gradually learnt to be friends with Buzz Lightyear. Buzz Lightyear did not know what he was made for, knowing he was a toy - he was some of Sid's victims. As toys with feelings, it would look upsetting to see another one of you be taken part & made into something cursed. By the way, Sid is my favourite Pixar villain: I like how he is a rebel & he ruined other people's items. My favourite quote from Woody: "This is Ludacris".

This is my favorite Pixar movie franchise. Sheriff Woody is my favorite character because he's relatable and quite handsome. I have two Sheriff Woody toys, one being a giant plush with a hard material and the other being entirely made of hard material and the latter version is missing it's legs but I never intentionally break my toys. My favorite toys from childhood are my Thomas the Tank Engine toys and my Bob the Builder toys. I have a toy Scoop which my sister broke and that made me very upset. Anyway back to the movie, I'm definitely not like Sid and I wouldn't want my toys handled by him. I can understand how Woody is feeling about Buzz being Andy's potentially new favorite toy. Obviously one of the greatest moves of all time. It’s a masterpiece; a technological marvel that broke through the animation medium and created a set of lovable, iconic characters that remain timeless to this day. Its story is a simple but effective one, about rediscovering one’s roots and the meaning of friendship and acceptance. It just as this universal appeal, something that everyone can understand and love about it. It’s also infinitely rewatchable, being able to pack an emotional punch without sacrificing its entertainment value and sense of humor. It’s a classic for a reason, and remains one of the greatest animated films of all time.

THEY DID THE BEST THEY COULD.

Toy Story is one of the best childhood movies of all time. It was revolutionary as the first feature-length film made entirely with 3D computer animation. Woody and Buzz Lightyear were my favorite toys, and their iconic lines—Buzz’s "To infinity… and beyond!" and Woody’s "Reach for the sky!"—are unforgettable. Woody, a kind-hearted cowboy doll, feels threatened when Buzz becomes Andy’s new favorite toy. Buzz believes he’s a real space ranger, and together they must escape the dangerous neighbor Sid and reunite with Andy after the family moves.

★★★★★ Toy Story is pure, undiluted magic: the movie that didn’t just launch Pixar, it invented modern animated storytelling and broke every heart in the room while making us laugh until we cried. From the very first moment you hear "You’ve got a friend in me" over those cloud wallpaper credits, you’re done for. This is a film that takes a child’s simple question ("What if my toys are alive when I’m not looking?") and turns it into a profound, hilarious, heartbreaking meditation on identity, jealousy, purpose, and the terror of being replaced. Tom Hanks’ Woody is the ultimate anxious leader trying to hold his world together. Tim Allen’s Buzz Lightyear is delusion, courage, and vulnerability wrapped in the greatest character introduction in animation history ("To infinity… and beyond!" still gives goosebumps thirty years later). Their rivalry-to-friendship arc is so perfectly written that even as an adult you feel every sting of jealousy and every rush of reconciliation. And then there’s the sheer invention: the claw machine aliens, the mutant toys in Sid’s room (still nightmare fuel), that pizza-planet truck chase, the rocket finale that somehow manages to be the most thrilling and emotional climax of the 90s. Randy Newman’s soundtrack isn’t just iconic; it’s woven into the DNA of childhood itself. Toy Story isn’t just a kids’ movie. It’s the first film that looked at children’s toys and saw real souls inside them, and in doing so taught an entire generation how to feel empathy for literally anything. Timeless. Revolutionary. Perfect. You don’t outgrow Toy Story. You just learn new ways to cry at it every time you rewatch. 10/10. To infinity and beyond… and straight into my heart forever. 🧸🚀❤️
